frameworks.name: PFC visual perception
frameworks.one_line: PFC as integral substrate for visual perception and recognition, not only task readout

frameworks.name: top-down attention
frameworks.one_line: traditional view that PFC feature encoding only serves behaviorally relevant functions

frameworks.name: recognition memory
frameworks.one_line: PFC contribution to recognizing visual objects drawn from human lesion and imaging work

core_question: Does prefrontal cortex play an integral role in visual perception and recognition rather than merely serving behaviorally relevant readout?
key_assumptions: PFC neuronal activity reflects visual features
evidence can be triangulated across primate single-unit, human neuroimaging/lesion, and AI models
psychiatric disorders inform PFC-visual function links
manipulations: perspective varied across symposium talks (nonhuman primate tuning, human neuroimaging, human lesions, AI models)
key_findings: PFC neuronal activity reflects visual features
emerging evidence challenges the view that PFC feature encoding is only for behaviorally relevant functions
interpretations: PFC may be integral for visual perception and recognition, not merely a downstream readout
convergence across primate, human, and AI evidence has implications for psychiatric disorders
limitations_noted: symposium overview rather than a single empirical study
